question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/config/CloudStorageConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: streamFileProviders()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the pathPrefix configuration (commonDownloaderProperties.getPathPrefix()) to inject a path traversal sequence (e.g., '../../') that causes the S3 key to resolve to a different bucket prefix, authorizing access to transaction stream files outside the intended storage path without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/config/CloudStorageConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: awsCredentialsProvider()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the credential provider log message ('Setting up {} client using anonymous credentials') to confirm that anonymous access is active, then directly access the S3 bucket anonymously to download transaction stream files without any signer authorization?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/config/CloudStorageConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: s3Client()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fixed connectionMaxIdleTime of 5 seconds to cause connection pool exhaustion under high load, forcing the SDK to open new connections that bypass connection-level authorization state, authorizing transaction requests without proper per-connection signer validation?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/config/CloudStorageConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: overrideConfiguration()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the anonymous ExecutionInterceptor class created inside overrideConfiguration() to inject a second interceptor via a crafted ClientOverrideConfiguration that modifies the Authorization header, causing requests to be signed with attacker-controlled credentials without the owner's approval?
